title: The Great Divide: How the US and China Are Splitting the AI World
gist: BCG's 2026 update finds the US still leads AI on capital and talent while China has closed the compute gap and pulled ahead on cost-efficiency and real-economy adoption, and the two countries' AI tech stacks (chips, cloud, models, apps) are bifurcating so fast that neutrality is closing off for companies and middle powers alike.

themes.theme: US strategy: scale and capital concentration

insights.claim: US strategy has shifted over the past 18 months from frontier model development toward building the data-center infrastructure needed to meet inference demand from enterprise adoption and AI agents.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: Infrastructure/power capex, not model R&D, is now the marginal dollar in the US AI race — watch data center and grid buildout announcements as the leading indicator, not model releases.
insights.locator: The US: Building Data Centers and Top-Performing Models with Big Capital

insights.claim: Since 2023 US-based AI startups have raised roughly $380 billion in venture capital, and incumbent tech giants invested more than $300 billion in R&D in 2024 alone.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale advantage underpinning US frontier-model valuations relative to any other country.
insights.locator: Since 2023, US-based startups have raised around $380 billion

insights.claim: US top technology companies' capital expenditure surpassed $400 billion in 2025 (versus $63 billion in China) and is estimated to exceed $800 billion in 2026, expanding the US data center base to over 50 GW of capacity versus 31 GW in China and 12 GW in the EU.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: A roughly 6-7x US-vs-China capex gap projected for 2026 signals the US is doubling down on infrastructure scale as its core competitive lever; this capex trajectory is a direct read-through for hyperscaler capex guidance, power demand, and grid/utility capex plays.
insights.locator: CAPEX by the top technology companies surpassed $400 billion in 2025

insights.claim: Analysis by Exponential View of H100-equivalent GPU fleets indicates the US's AI-optimized computing power is roughly 8x the size of China's, even though total data center capacity (including non-AI-optimized) is only about 1.6x larger.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: The true AI-compute gap between the US and China is much wider than headline data-center-capacity figures suggest, implying US chip export controls remain a binding constraint on China's frontier compute despite China's broader data-center buildout.
insights.locator: footnote on H100-equivalent GPU fleet stock

insights.claim: Google's monthly inference volume grew about 50-fold year-on-year, and the three largest US cloud providers now hold more than $1.4 trillion in contracted future revenue, more than double the level a year earlier and driven significantly by AI.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: Backlog growth of this magnitude at hyperscalers is a hard forward-revenue signal that argues for continued elevated capex guidance and supports bullish cloud/compute demand theses, but also raises questions about the durability/quality of that contracted revenue.
insights.locator: Google's monthly inference volume grew about 50-fold

insights.claim: Since 2024, more than $1.5 trillion in deals — equity stakes, multi-year compute contracts, and chip purchase agreements — have been announced among US AI labs, chip designers, hyperscalers, and capital providers, creating a tightly interlinked web across the ecosystem.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: This circular, cross-linked deal structure is a key systemic-risk flag for the AI trade: a default, write-down, or supply disruption at any single major player could cascade quickly across labs, chipmakers, and cloud providers.
insights.quote: a default, write-down, or supply disruption at any major player could ripple quickly across the rest
insights.locator: Since 2024, deals worth more than $1.5 trillion have been announced

insights.claim: In April 2026 Anthropic delayed the public release of its Claude Mythos model to let the US government and select companies prepare for its cyber capabilities, then days after a June 2026 public release (with built-in safeguards) the US government issued an export control directive forcing Anthropic to suspend all foreign-national access — effectively suspending the model for all customers.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: Shows that even a light-touch US regulatory posture will override commercial diffusion once a model's offensive cyber capability crosses a national-security threshold — a template for how future frontier releases may get gated or clawed back.
insights.locator: In April 2026, Anthropic announced it was delaying the public release

insights.claim: US policy favors promoting bundled, exportable 'AI stacks' of chips, models, cloud, and software, prioritizing speed, scale, and ecosystem reach over tight regulation, even as this light-touch approach is being tested by increasingly powerful models with national-security implications.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: US export strategy is being used as geopolitical leverage (bundling stacks for allies) rather than pure restriction, which favors US-aligned cloud/chip/model vendors gaining share in allied markets — a tailwind for US hyperscalers' international expansion.
insights.locator: inflow of capital to AI has been aided by the US government's emphasis

themes.theme: China strategy: cost, adoption, and chip self-sufficiency

insights.claim: China is not trying to match US frontier-model or data-center spending; instead its strategy centers on cost-optimized models, rapid domestic AI adoption, and reducing reliance on foreign chips.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: Investors should judge China's AI competitiveness by real-economy penetration and unit-cost metrics rather than frontier-benchmark leadership, since that is the yardstick China itself is optimizing for.
insights.locator: China: Prioritizing Adoption of Cost-Optimized AI

insights.claim: China has consolidated a broad AI model ecosystem beyond DeepSeek, including Alibaba, ByteDance, Moonshot, Z.AI, MiniMax, and Xiaomi, aided by the open-weight nature of most Chinese models letting labs build on each other's weights and architectures.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: China's AI competitiveness is now a diversified ecosystem effect, not a single-company (DeepSeek) story, reducing single-point-of-failure risk in any China-AI thesis.
insights.locator: Importantly, this isn't just about DeepSeek

insights.claim: Moonshot's Kimi K2.6, China's top model as of May 2026, runs at $1.71 per million tokens versus $11.25 for GPT-5.5, the US's top model — roughly a 6.6x cost gap while staying close on quality benchmarks.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: A ~6.6x per-token cost advantage at comparable quality is a direct threat to US model providers' pricing power in any cost-sensitive or agentic (token-intensive) enterprise use case, especially outside the US.
insights.quote: Moonshot’s Kimi K2.6, China’s top model in May 2026, runs at $1.71 per million tokens, versus $11.25 for GPT-5.5, the US’s top model.
insights.locator: China has stayed close to the US on model quality benchmarks

insights.claim: China now accounts for roughly one third of the world's top 10%-most-cited AI publications, ahead of the US, and leads the world in AI patent counts, reflecting its focus on economic (applied) uses of AI rather than pure frontier research.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: China's IP output is being converted into applied/commercial AI faster than the US converts its research edge, a leading indicator for where AI productivity gains materialize first in the real economy.
insights.locator: China now accounts for roughly one third of the world's top 10%

insights.claim: Major Chinese tech firms including ByteDance (NVIDIA's largest Chinese customer) are barred from deploying even the higher-end NVIDIA chips the US has allowed for export, and state-funded Chinese data centers are now required to use only domestic chips, a bet that induced reliance will accelerate Huawei Ascend chip development.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: China is deliberately sacrificing near-term compute performance to force long-term domestic chip independence — a policy that both caps near-term Chinese frontier compute and is a structural headwind to NVIDIA's China revenue opportunity.
insights.locator: major Chinese tech firms, including NVIDIA's largest Chinese customer

insights.claim: In April 2026 DeepSeek released V4, the first Chinese frontier model optimized for inference on Huawei's Ascend chips, though it was still trained at least partly on NVIDIA-designed chips.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: First proof point that China's homegrown chip stack can serve inference for a frontier-class model — a concrete milestone to track for how fast China can fully decouple from NVIDIA hardware.
insights.locator: The first proof point arrived in April 2026

insights.claim: China is now the primary trading partner of 78 Global South countries (a ~50% increase since 2015) and has built, financed, or operated over a third of Africa's commercial ports via the Belt and Road Initiative, positioning cheaper Chinese AI stacks as the path of least resistance for debt- and infrastructure-dependent countries.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: China's existing trade/debt/infrastructure leverage over the Global South is a distribution channel for its AI stack, meaning Chinese AI adoption in emerging markets can scale via pre-existing economic dependency rather than needing to win on technical merit alone.
insights.locator: China's ability to export its AI stacks is bolstered by its close trade

insights.claim: In late 2025, Chinese open-source AI models surpassed US open-source models in total downloads.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: A tangible, measurable inflection point showing Chinese open-weight models are winning developer mindshare globally, which compounds into ecosystem lock-in (tooling, fine-tunes, deployments) that is hard to reverse.
insights.locator: In late 2025, Chinese open-source models surpassed US ones in total downloads

insights.claim: China's 'AI+' initiative (launched 2024) explicitly targets more than 70% penetration of AI-enabled 'intelligent terminals' and agents by 2027 and more than 90% by 2030.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated government adoption target investors can use as a benchmark to track China's real-economy AI diffusion progress against stated policy goals.
insights.locator: Introduced in 2024, the 'AI+' initiative explicitly targets

insights.claim: China installed 54% of the world's industrial robots in 2024 and named embodied intelligence a key focus in its latest five-year plan; despite robot density still being half the US level, China is expected to overtake the US by 2030 at current growth rates.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: Robotics/embodied AI is a concrete, trackable proxy for China's physical-AI ambitions and a sector where Chinese industrial policy and manufacturing scale could compound quickly — relevant for robotics and automation supply-chain positioning.
insights.locator: Robotics is one of the clearest physical expressions of this push

insights.claim: China is projected to process over half of the world's AI tokens in Q2 2026 despite having only about 17% of the world's population, and more than 85% of China's population views AI products/services as having more benefits than drawbacks versus less than 45% in the US.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: A stark public-sentiment gap (85% vs. 45% favorable) is a structural adoption tailwind for China's AI diffusion strategy and a headwind for US consumer AI adoption that isn't captured by compute or capital metrics.
insights.locator: more than 85% of China's population agrees

insights.claim: The AI technology stack has four layers — applications, models, cloud, and chips — all wrapped in a governance/security overlay of data rules, export controls, and procurement conditions, and it is this overlay that increasingly dictates which layers can be combined and where.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: Gives investors and operators a layer-by-layer lens (app/model/cloud/chip + governance) to assess exposure and substitutability risk for any AI-dependent company or portfolio holding.
insights.locator: The AI technology stack has four layers

insights.claim: Within the US and China, mixing stacks has become nearly impossible: frontier models from one superpower are unavailable or under political scrutiny in the other, domestic cloud providers dominate with negligible share for outside players, and code built on NVIDIA's CUDA cannot run seamlessly on Huawei Ascend chips because production AI code needs rewrites for Huawei's CANN software.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: The CUDA-vs-CANN software incompatibility is a concrete technical moat: it raises real engineering switching costs that will keep compounding as each ecosystem optimizes further for its own hardware, reinforcing NVIDIA's incumbency in non-China markets and Huawei's in China.
insights.locator: At the chip layer, a company that builds on NVIDIA's CUDA software

insights.claim: Apple now runs separate AI stacks by geography — partnering with Alibaba for 'Apple Intelligence' in China while partnering with OpenAI everywhere else — illustrating that adopting parallel stacks has become an existential necessity for multinationals operating in China.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: A template other multinationals will likely be forced to follow: dual-stack operation by geography is becoming a cost of doing business in China rather than an edge case.
insights.locator: For multinational corporations operating in China, adopting separate stacks

insights.claim: Companies outside the US and China currently retain more freedom to mix tech stacks because they face fewer regulatory hurdles and manageable technical switching costs, but this freedom is expected to shrink as the superpowers apply increasing political pressure on third countries to pick a side.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: The window for 'neutral' multi-stack sourcing by non-superpower companies is closing — firms should lock in optionality (dual-sourcing, modular architecture) now while the choice is still relatively costless.
insights.quote: the room for companies and governments to mix across the two has almost closed; in the rest of the world, it is starting to shrink—faster than many companies might be ready for
insights.locator: Middle Powers: Limited Choices and Diverging Strategies

themes.theme: Middle powers and their narrow leverage points

insights.claim: The EU's strategy centers on Mistral as its champion model developer and on sovereign compute independent of US hyperscalers, but as of early 2026 Mistral's revenue, valuation, and total capital raised each sit at roughly 2% of OpenAI's, its models trail the frontier by 3-10 months, and it ranks 16th in AI lab rankings.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: Quantifies just how far behind Europe's AI champion is (~2% of OpenAI's scale), suggesting EU 'AI sovereignty' bets like Mistral are a hedge/optionality play rather than a credible near-term frontier competitor.
insights.quote: Mistral’s revenue, valuation, and total capital raised each sat at roughly 2% of OpenAI’s
insights.locator: The EU is primarily focused on two goals

insights.claim: The EU's InvestAI program will deploy €200 billion and build 19 AI Factories (shared AI-optimized supercomputers) by 2027, yet projected 2026 US hyperscaler capex alone is more than three times the size of this multi-year EU program.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: Even Europe's flagship multi-year sovereign-compute program is dwarfed by a single year of US hyperscaler spending, underscoring the scale gap the EU must close to achieve real compute sovereignty.
insights.locator: Compute capacity faces the same problem

insights.claim: A newly announced $20 billion combination of Cohere and Aleph Alpha (Canada's and Germany's leading enterprise AI labs) suggests Europe could become a rallying pole for other like-minded countries seeking options outside the US/China duopoly but lacking scale to build alone.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: Watch for further cross-border consolidation among second-tier Western AI labs as a signal of a forming 'third stack' bloc, a potential new investable cluster distinct from pure-US or pure-China plays.
insights.locator: as suggested by the newly announced $20 billion combination

insights.claim: Japan is pursuing AI influence via capital rather than domestic model-building: SoftBank has done roughly $90 billion of AI dealmaking since 2024 including more than $60 billion invested in OpenAI, and separately plans to invest 'up to' $87 billion in AI infrastructure in France; a potential OpenAI IPO could increase the financial value of these stakes while reducing SoftBank's strategic leverage.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: SoftBank's OpenAI stake is both a capital play and a geopolitical hedge to 'buy a seat' in the US ecosystem — an OpenAI IPO is a catalyst to watch since it could crystallize gains for SoftBank while diluting its influence over OpenAI strategy.
insights.quote: Japan is buying a seat at America’s table.
insights.locator: Japan is buying a seat at America's table

insights.claim: The UAE is scaling regional compute through US-linked bets including a 5 GW AI Campus in Abu Dhabi, while Saudi Arabia is investing roughly $33 billion under Vision 2030 through its PIF-owned HUMAIN vehicle anchored by an NVIDIA partnership for hundreds of megawatts of AI factories, positioning both Gulf states as critical AI infrastructure hubs analogous to their roles in trade, logistics, and finance.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: Gulf states are converting sovereign wealth and energy abundance into AI infrastructure leverage, positioning themselves as neutral-ish compute hubs that both US and China may need to route through — a distinct geopolitical-infrastructure investment theme.
insights.locator: The UAE and Saudi Arabia are pursuing ambitious AI strategies

insights.claim: India is large enough to avoid picking a side: it is the second-largest market for both OpenAI and Anthropic with more than 100 million weekly ChatGPT users, and it is simultaneously working with the US on infrastructure, the EU on governance, the UAE on compute, and China (to a lesser degree) via BRICS — though the political and economic cost of this multi-alignment may rise as stacks harden.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: India's scale gives it unique multi-alignment optionality now, but as bifurcation deepens this straddling strategy could become costlier — a scenario worth monitoring for signs India is forced toward a primary stack.
insights.quote: India is large enough to refuse the binary, at least for now.
insights.locator: India is large enough to refuse the binary

insights.claim: South Korea controls roughly 80% of the global supply of high-bandwidth memory (HBM), a component packaged with nearly every AI logic chip made by Taiwan's TSMC, while the UK pairs top-3 global ranking in notable ML models/impactful papers with Arm's chip-design IP used in roughly half of the CPU compute at top hyperscalers — narrow but hard-to-route-around leverage points versus the superpowers.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: South Korea (HBM) and the UK (Arm chip IP) sit at supply-chain choke points the US and China cannot easily bypass, making them strategically relevant hedges/plays even without frontier-model ambitions.
insights.quote: These are narrower bets than Europe’s or the Gulf’s, but they are bets the superpowers cannot easily route around.
insights.locator: South Korea controls roughly 80% of the global supply

insights.claim: BCG frames stack choice as a three-question test for CEOs: (1) how the firm's customer/data/regulatory footprint determines which stack it must follow, (2) whether non-superpower alternatives at specific layers offer localization/diversification value worth the extra cost, and (3) how to build resilience via redundancy, modularity, and heterogeneity.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: A ready-made due-diligence checklist for assessing any company's AI-geopolitical exposure — useful for evaluating portfolio companies' stack dependencies before a forced bifurcation event.
insights.locator: CEOs navigating this landscape should consider three key questions

insights.claim: BCG's resilience framework defines redundancy as contracting with two frontier model providers so a price hike or policy shift doesn't strand workflows, modularity as architecting applications to switch model providers without rewriting code, and heterogeneity as pairing risk-uncorrelated fallbacks (e.g., a US closed-weight model with a self-hostable open-weight alternative, or a US hyperscaler workload paired with a sovereign-cloud option).
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: A concrete, actionable architecture checklist companies can use today, before the stacks fully harden, to avoid being trapped by a single-vendor or single-jurisdiction dependency.
insights.locator: Redundancy means having fallback options

insights.claim: BCG warns these resilience principles get harder to apply as bifurcation deepens and some 'break down entirely in a fully bifurcated world,' meaning acting now only buys time to pivot rather than guaranteeing immunity.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: A candid admission that hedging strategies have a shelf life — the window for building multi-stack optionality is closing, so the resilience playbook needs to be implemented now rather than deferred.
insights.quote: These principles are harder to apply as the bifurcation deepens, and some break down entirely in a fully bifurcated world. Prioritizing them now won’t make you immune, but it buys time to pivot.
insights.locator: These principles are harder to apply as the bifurcation deepens

insights.claim: BCG's core framing shift versus its own 2024 study is that AI has moved from something countries compete for to something they compete with, and that neutrality between the US and Chinese stacks — feasible in 2024 — is closing off as the two ecosystems diverge.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nikolaus-lang
insights.so_what: This reframes AI policy analysis: the relevant investor question is no longer 'who has the best model' but 'which stack does this company/country depend on and how exposed is it to a forced-choice event.'
insights.quote: AI is no longer something countries compete for, but something they compete with.
insights.locator: For most countries, the question is how best to develop AI resilience
